title: Deduplicate identical orchestrator cards when sharing task tree
analysis:
The bug: discover_orchestrators() loads the task tree once and attaches the identical tasks/worktrees/summary to every process entry. When two orchestrator processes run against the same PRD, the dashboard renders two visually identical cards — same progress bar, same task list, same stats — differing only by PID. This is confusing.

Root cause in orchestrator.py lines 249-256: the function iterates over all processes and blindly spreads each proc dict (**proc) into the result, attaching the shared task tree to each. There is no grouping by PRD.

Fix approach (Option 3 from task description): Group processes by PRD path in discover_orchestrators(), merging PIDs into a 'pids' list. Produce one entry per unique PRD. The template then conditionally renders 'PID 1234' (singular) or 'PIDs 1234, 5678' (plural).

Key observations:
- find_running_orchestrators() returns dicts with 'pid' (int), 'prd' (str), 'running' (bool), 'started' (str)
- discover_orchestrators() spreads proc dict via **proc into result entries
- Template references orch.pid (singular) on line 12
- MOCK_ORCHESTRATOR_RUNNING/COMPLETED in test_orchestrator_panel.py use 'pid' key
- 4 existing TestDiscoverOrchestrators tests assert entry['pid']
- find_running_orchestrators() stays unchanged — it correctly reports raw per-process data
- Grouping is a pure data transformation in discover_orchestrators()
- 'running' for a group should be True if ANY constituent process is running (OR semantics)
- 'started' is display-only from ps — use first entry's value

steps.description: Write test test_single_process_produces_pids_list in TestDiscoverOrchestrators: mock one running orchestrator, call discover_orchestrators, assert result[0] has 'pids' key containing [1234], and assert 'pid' key is absent from result[0].

Refactor discover_orchestrators() to group processes by PRD: use groups = {} / groups.setdefault(proc['prd'], []).append(proc). Iterate over groups instead of individual processes. For each unique PRD, emit one entry with 'pids': [p['pid'] for p in group], 'prd': prd, 'running': any(p['running'] for p in group), 'started': group[0]['started'], plus shared tasks/worktrees/summary. Also update the 4 existing TestDiscoverOrchestrators tests to use entry['pids'] instead of entry['pid']: test_combines_process_and_worktree_data, test_missing_tasks_json, test_worktree_keyed_by_task_id_not_dirname, test_non_task_worktree_dirs_excluded.

Write test test_same_prd_grouped_into_single_entry in TestDiscoverOrchestrators: mock find_running_orchestrators returning two processes with the same PRD path but different PIDs (1234 and 5678, both prd='/home/leo/prd.md'). Create tasks.json and a worktree. Assert len(result)==1. Assert result[0]['pids']==[1234, 5678]. Assert tasks/worktrees/summary present once.
Verify step-3 test passes with existing grouping logic from step-2. No new production code expected — setdefault grouping already handles same-PRD merging. Fix if needed.

Write test test_different_prds_produce_separate_entries: mock two processes with different PRD paths (/prd1.md and /prd2.md). Assert len(result)==2, each with pids list of length 1. Write test test_grouped_running_true_when_any_running: two procs same PRD, one running=True one running=False, assert entry['running']==True. Write test test_grouped_running_false_when_all_stopped: two procs same PRD both running=False, assert entry['running']==False.
Verify step-5 tests pass with existing logic. The any() call for running and dict key grouping should cover all cases. Fix if needed.

Update test_orchestrator_panel.py mock data: change MOCK_ORCHESTRATOR_RUNNING from 'pid': 1234 to 'pids': [1234], and MOCK_ORCHESTRATOR_COMPLETED from 'pid': 5678 to 'pids': [5678]. Add new test class TestOrchestratorMultiPid with MOCK_ORCHESTRATOR_MULTI_PID constant (pids: [1234, 5678], same PRD, same tasks/worktrees/summary as RUNNING). Tests: (1) test_multi_pid_header_shows_pids_plural — 'PIDs' in html + both '1234' and '5678'; (2) test_single_pid_header_shows_pid_singular — 'PID ' in html for single-PID entry; (3) test_count_badge_shows_one_for_grouped — count badge '1' for single grouped entry with multiple PIDs; (4) test_multi_pid_comma_separated — pids=[111, 222, 333] renders '111, 222, 333'.

Update orchestrators.html template line 12: replace 'PID {{ orch.pid }}' with conditional: {% if orch.pids|length > 1 %}PIDs {{ orch.pids|join(', ') }}{% else %}PID {{ orch.pids[0] }}{% endif %}. Verify ALL panel tests pass including new multi-PID tests and all existing tests with updated mock data.
Run full test suite (pytest dashboard/tests/) to verify no regressions. Check that existing tests in test_app.py, test_badge_macros.py, and other test files still pass. Fix any issues.

design_decisions.rationale: Clean break over backward compatibility. The only consumer is the orchestrators.html template (also modified in this task), so there is no external API contract to maintain. A list is the natural representation whether there's 1 or N processes. Keeping both 'pid' and 'pids' would invite confusion about which to use.

design_decisions.decision: Group by exact PRD path string
design_decisions.rationale: Simplest correct matching. PRD paths come from the same ps aux regex parsing, so identical processes will have byte-identical path strings. Path normalization (resolve, expanduser) adds complexity without benefit since orchestrator invocations use consistent paths.

design_decisions.decision: Set grouped 'running' to any(p['running'] for p in group)
design_decisions.rationale: If any process in the group is still running, the orchestration is active. This is the most informative signal for the dashboard user. The status badge should show green/running as long as at least one process is alive.

design_decisions.decision: Keep 'started' as first process's value, not min/max
design_decisions.rationale: The template does not currently render 'started'. Lexicographic comparison of ps time formats (e.g. 'Mar18' vs '10:30') is unreliable across different ps output conventions. Using group[0]['started'] is simple and sufficient.

design_decisions.decision: Inline grouping in discover_orchestrators() rather than a separate _group_by_prd() helper
design_decisions.rationale: The grouping logic is 5-6 lines (setdefault + list iteration + any()). Extracting a helper would add a function boundary, docstring, and test class for trivial logic. The discover_orchestrators tests already cover the grouping behavior end-to-end. If the logic grows, it can be extracted later.

Separation of concerns: find_running_orchestrators() reports raw per-process data faithfully. discover_orchestrators() is the aggregation/enrichment layer where grouping belongs. This keeps the process-scanning function simple and independently testable.

design_decisions.decision: Singular/plural PID label via Jinja2 conditional on pids|length
design_decisions.rationale: 'PID 1234' reads naturally for single process; 'PIDs 1234, 5678' clearly communicates shared work. The |join filter handles any number of PIDs. No new macros or filters needed — reuses existing Jinja2 patterns in the template.

reuse.how: Unchanged — still returns per-process dicts. Grouping is applied to its output in discover_orchestrators().
Reused unchanged for all panel tests — patches discover_orchestrators at app.py import location. Mock data just needs 'pids' instead of 'pid'.
No changes needed — badge rendering for running/completed status is independent of PID grouping.

New tests follow the same pattern: tmp_path fixtures, mock find_running_orchestrators, call discover_orchestrators(config), assert result structure. Only change: assertions use entry['pids'] (list) instead of entry['pid'] (int).
Template already uses |length for count badge. PID display uses the same |length for conditional and adds |join for comma-separated rendering.
